Client 2: Yeah, and we want to be precise about what’s fixed versus flexible, and what to do with savings versus debt.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow, then we’ll map assets and debts. Patrici, what’s your household gross income per year—ballpark is fine, but if you know the exact number, even better.
Client 1: The exact household gross is 178950 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, what are your total monthly expenses all-in right now?
Client 1: Total monthly expenses are 14150 a month.

Client 1: The exact household gross is 178950 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, what are your total monthly expenses all-in right now?
Client 1: Total monthly expenses are 14150 a month.
Advisor: Okay. When you say that, does that include the debt payments, like the mortgage and any other loans, or is that separate?
Client 1: That includes everything—rent—sorry, mortgage—utilities, food, all of it.

Client 1: That includes everything—rent—sorry, mortgage—utilities, food, all of it.
Advisor: Perfect, thanks for correcting that—mortgage. Let’s isolate the debt piece for a second. Michael, roughly what are the monthly debt payments total across everything?
Client 2: Monthly debt payments total is 5550.
Advisor: Okay—so that’s the mortgage plus the other loan. Can you break those two out just so I understand what’s driving it?
Client 2: The mortgage is 5350 per month, and then the other loan is 200 per month.

Client 2: Balance outstanding is 3000, interest rate is 28.18%, and final payment date is 2030-03-21.
Advisor: Oof—28.18% is spicy. We’ll come back to prioritization. Switching to assets: across your investable accounts—so not the home—about how much do you have total?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 191800.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s unpack what makes up that 191800 so I’m not guessing. What accounts are you thinking of there?
Client 1: There’s a joint taxable account—well, I call it taxable—held through an insurance company, and it’s 107250.

Client 2: Yeah, there’s another one held at a retirement platform—27,000.
Advisor: Got it. And then you also have the home—what are you using as the current property value?
Client 1: Property value is 83450.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to confirm, that home value is separate from the investable assets number you gave me earlier, right?
Client 2: Correct—191800 is investable only; the 83450 is the property value.

Client 2: Yep—mortgage balance outstanding is 57400, and the other loan balance outstanding is 3000.
Advisor: Great. And when you think about “loans outstanding” as a household total—so mortgage plus any other loans—what number should I be using?
Client 1: Loans outstanding total is 60400.
Advisor: Perfect—so that 60400 is basically those two added together. Patrici, on the property side, you’d said the home value earlier—what are you using there again?
Client 1: Property value is 83450.

Client 2: The mortgage is 5350 per month, and then the other loan is 200 per month.
Advisor: Thanks. And just so I capture the mortgage details correctly—what’s the current balance and the interest rate?
Client 2: Mortgage balance outstanding is 57400, and the interest rate is 5.42%.
Advisor: And when is that scheduled to be fully paid off—what’s the final payment date you’re seeing?
Client 2: Final payment date is 2034-08-28.

Client 2: Final payment date is 2034-08-28.
Advisor: Great. Now the other loan—what’s the balance, rate, and the final payoff date on that one?
Client 2: Balance outstanding is 3000, interest rate is 28.18%, and final payment date is 2030-03-21.
Advisor: Oof—28.18% is spicy. We’ll come back to prioritization. Switching to assets: across your investable accounts—so not the home—about how much do you have total?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 191800.

Advisor: Hi Patrici, hi Michael—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, it’s the monthly burn rate—like, it feels high—and we want a plan that doesn’t make us feel behind.
Client 2: Yeah, and we want to be precise about what’s fixed versus flexible, and what to do with savings versus debt.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow, then we’ll map assets and debts. Patrici, what’s your household gross income per year—ballpark is fine, but if you know the exact number, even better.
Client 1: The exact household gross is 178950 annually.

Advisor: Perfect, I’ll keep it at Other.
Advisor: And then taxes—when you think about your marginal bracket, what band are you generally landing in right now?
Client 2: We’re in the 35% bracket band.
Advisor: Okay—35%. Thanks.
Advisor: While I’ve got you: earlier you said no life insurance or disability outside of work. Do you want me to just leave protection as “through work only” for now and we can detail it later if needed?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, what are your total monthly expenses all-in right now?
Client 1: Total monthly expenses are 14150 a month.
Advisor: Okay. When you say that, does that include the debt payments, like the mortgage and any other loans, or is that separate?
Client 1: That includes everything—rent—sorry, mortgage—utilities, food, all of it.
Advisor: Perfect, thanks for correcting that—mortgage. Let’s isolate the debt piece for a second. Michael, roughly what are the monthly debt payments total across everything?

Advisor: Patrici, is it just the two of you in the household—so 2 adults?
Client 1: Yes, 2 adults—just me and Michael.
Advisor: Great. And marital status—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. Next one’s a little unglamorous—what state are you filing from? You don’t have to tell me the exact state name if you’d rather not; I can also note it as “Other” if that’s how you track it.

Advisor: While I’ve got you: earlier you said no life insurance or disability outside of work. Do you want me to just leave protection as “through work only” for now and we can detail it later if needed?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s fine—nothing outside of work right now.
Advisor: Great. And just so I’m not missing anything, income-wise we’ve got your household gross income at 178950 per year—does that include any bonus or variable comp you’re expecting this year?
Client 2: Yes, that 178950 includes it—there’s a bonus component in there.
Advisor: Okay, perfect. And on assets, we already captured the investable total at 191800 and the property value at 83450—so we’ll keep those buckets separate like we discussed.

Client 2: Yeah, and we want to be precise about what’s fixed versus flexible, and what to do with savings versus debt.

Client 2: No—0 dependents.
Advisor: Okay. When you think about investing and big money decisions, how would you describe your risk comfort—conservative, moderate, aggressive…?
Client 1: I’d say moderate.
Advisor: Okay, moderate. Michael, same for you, or do you see it differently?

Client 2: Yep—Client 2.
Advisor: Perfect. Now income for you specifically, Michael—what’s your gross annual income before taxes and deductions?
Client 2: It’s 84800 gross annually.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And what line of work are you in—broadly speaking? Like tech, education, healthcare, that kind of level.
Client 2: Healthcare.

Advisor: Perfect. Now income for you specifically, Michael—what’s your gross annual income before taxes and deductions?
Client 2: It’s 84800 gross annually.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And what line of work are you in—broadly speaking? Like tech, education, healthcare, that kind of level.
Client 2: Healthcare.
Advisor: Great—thank you. Patrici, I’m going to come back to you next for your side, but before I do—Michael, is that 84800 basically salary, or does it swing a lot?

Advisor: And protection: nothing outside of work right now—so we’ll leave it as through work only for the moment.
Client 2: Yes, through work only.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. I want to zoom in on each of you for a second so the plan isn’t just one blended number. Michael, can we start with your role in the household—are you the spouse/partner on the plan?
Client 2: Yes, I’m the spouse/partner.
Advisor: Got it. And just to make sure I’m labeling you correctly in my notes—Michael, you’re Client 2, right?
